/**
 * SPA entry — wires `api`, `router`, and the DOM into a file-browser UI
 * rendered inside a native `<dialog>` widget.
 *
 * Structure (built imperatively via `document.createElement`):
 *
 *   .file-browser-host   — primary chromeless surface; hosts the widget while
 *                          the dialog is closed (embedded view)
 *   <button class="trigger">Browse Files</button>
 *   <dialog class="browser-dialog">  — frame + titlebar (dialog-only chrome)
 *     .dialog-header     — title <span> + Close icon <button>
 *     .dialog-body        — empty slot; the shared widget moves here on open
 *
 *   The shared .file-browser widget (toolbar + .results + .status) is MOVED
 *   between the embedded host and the dialog body as the dialog opens/closes.
 *   It carries no border/background/title of its own — only the dialog adds
 *   the frame + titlebar, so the embedded view is chromeless.
 *
 *   .file-browser:
 *     toolbar   — .breadcrumb + search <input> + <button> + <label> upload
 *     .results  — file/folder <table>
 *     <footer class="status"> — summary line
 *
 * All user-controlled strings (file/folder names, paths) are inserted via
 * `textContent` / element creation — never via `innerHTML` — to prevent HTML
 * injection.
 */
import { ApiClient } from './api.js';
import type { BrowseResult, FileEntry, SearchResult } from './api.js';
import { parseHash, getCurrentRoute, subscribe, toBrowseHash, toSearchHash, navigate } from './router.js';
import type { Route } from './router.js';
import { formatBytes, normalizeRelativePath, joinPath, parentPath, basename, formatDate } from './format.js';

const api = new ApiClient();

```typescript
describe('formatDate (modified column)', () => {
    it('renders an empty Modified cell when lastModified is empty (fallback)', async () => {
      const entry = fileEntry({ name: 'nodate.txt', path: 'docs/nodate.txt', lastModified: '' });
      const { results } = await setupCleared();
      renderBrowse(browseResult({ path: 'docs', entries: [entry] }), browseRoute('docs'));

      const modifiedCell = cellsOf(rowByName(results.querySelector('table')!, 'nodate.txt')!)[2];
      expect(modifiedCell.textContent).toBe('');
    });
  });
```

Actually, let me re-check: is the joinPath test a near-duplicate? Let me compare:

Existing test (around line 338):
```
it('renders clickable segments that navigate to each cumulative path', async () => {
  const { breadcrumb } = await setupCleared();
  renderBrowse(browseResult({ path: 'a/b', entries: [] }), browseRoute('a/b'));

  const segB = leafByText(breadcrumb, 'b')! as HTMLElement;
  segB.click();
  expect(window.location.hash).toBe(toBrowseHash('a/b'));

  const segA = leafByText(breadcrumb, 'a')! as HTMLElement;
  segA.click();
  expect(window.location.hash).toBe(toBrowseHash('a'));
});
```

New test:
```
it('joins each cumulative segment across three path levels', async () => {
  const { breadcrumb } = await setupCleared();
  renderBrowse(browseResult({ path: 'a/b/c', entries: [] }), browseRoute('a/b/c'));

  leafByText(breadcrumb, 'c')!.click();
  expect(window.location.hash).toBe(toBrowseHash('a/b/c'));

  leafByText(breadcrumb, 'b')!.click();
  expect(window.location.hash).toBe(toBrowseHash('a/b'));
});
```

These are very similar - same pattern, just one adds a third level. It's a minor variation. The new test IS somewhat of a near-duplicate but adds the 3-level case. This is marginal.

For the "formatDate" test: the existing test uses `ISO_FMT = '2024-03-15 10:30'` to verify formatting. The new test only checks that empty input produces empty output. This is a useful edge case.

For "formatBytes": existing test checks `formatBytes(1536)` → '1.5 KB'. New test checks `formatBytes(0)` → '0 B'. Useful edge case.

For "toBrowseHash": The existing breadcrumb tests use `toBrowseHash` in assertions but don't test its encoding behavior. The new test specifically tests percent-encoding. Useful.

For "toSearchHash": The existing search button tests use `toSearchHash` in assertions. The new test specifically tests encoding of special characters. Useful.

For "normalizeRelativePath": The existing breadcrumb test with path 'a/b' doesn't test path normalization. The new test with '//a//b//' specifically tests that. Useful.

So overall: 5 out of 6 are genuinely adding new coverage. The joinPath one is a near-duplicate but still adds the 3-level case.

If an import were accidentally removed, the TypeScript build would fail before tests even run (since the module-level code that uses these functions would have a ReferenceError). So in a sense, the characterization tests add a small safety net but the main guard is the TypeScript build itself. The comment in the test code correctly notes this: "if that import were removed, the referenced call would throw (ReferenceError) or emit wrong output and the test would fail."

Looking at the "joinPath (breadcrumb cumulative paths)" test:
```typescript
it('joins each cumulative segment across three path levels', async () => {
  const { breadcrumb } = await setupCleared();
  renderBrowse(browseResult({ path: 'a/b/c', entries: [] }), browseRoute('a/b/c'));

  leafByText(breadcrumb, 'c')!.click();
  expect(window.location.hash).toBe(toBrowseHash('a/b/c'));

  leafByText(breadcrumb, 'b')!.click();
  expect(window.location.hash).toBe(toBrowseHash('a/b'));
});
```

This test is essentially the same as the existing breadcrumb test at line ~667:
```typescript
it('renders clickable segments that navigate to each cumulative path', async () => {
  const { breadcrumb } = await setupCleared();
  renderBrowse(browseResult({ path: 'a/b', entries: [] }), browseRoute('a/b'));

  const segB = leafByText(breadcrumb, 'b')! as HTMLElement;
  segB.click();
  expect(window.location.hash).toBe(toBrowseHash('a/b'));

  const segA = leafByText(breadcrumb, 'a')! as HTMLElement;
  segA.click();
  expect(window.location.hash).toBe(toBrowseHash('a'));
});
```

The new test adds one more level (3 vs 2 levels) which provides marginal additional coverage. This is fine.
